1: Hērōdēs ho basileus ('Herod the king'): Herod Agrippa I (10 BC–AD 44), grandson of Herod the Great and nephew of Herod Antipas (who executed John the Baptist). Raised in Rome as a companion of the imperial household — educated alongside the future emperors Caligula and Claudius — his social connections allowed him to acquire territorial titles piecemeal: Philip's tetrarchy (AD 37), Antipas's Galilee (AD 39), and finally Judea and Samaria from Claudius (AD 41), the first ruler since Herod the Great to control all Jewish territory. Josephus (Ant. 19.7-8) portrays him as popular with the Jewish people, scrupulous in Torah observance when in Jerusalem, and politically astute — the most credible Herod in Jewish memory.
2: Iakōbon ton adelphon Iōannou anheilen machairē ('he killed James the brother of John with the sword'): the first apostolic martyrdom in Acts. James and John, sons of Zebedee, had asked for the seats at Jesus's left and right in his glory (Mark 10:35-40); Jesus had responded with the cup-of-suffering prophecy. The sword execution — probably beheading — was within Herod's authority as a Jewish king for sedition. That James was executed first among the Twelve and Luke reports it in a single clause without theological elaboration is historically honest: no explanation is offered, no miracle rescues him. The contrast with Peter's miraculous escape heightens both the unexpectedness of the rescue and the reality of martyrdom in the same narrative.
3: Heorakas tōn azymōn ('Festival of Unleavened Bread'): the seven-day festival immediately following Passover (Leviticus 23:5-8). Jerusalem's population swelled multiple times over with pilgrims during this period; any public execution or trial would have maximum visibility. Herod's arrest of Peter during the festival — with a public trial planned afterward — suggests a calculated political spectacle timed to the religious calendar, the same strategy the Sanhedrin had used when plotting against Jesus (Mark 14:2, 'not during the feast'). The Passover/Unleavened Bread context also invites the Exodus typology that Luke develops through the angelic rescue of Acts 12:6-11.
4: Tetradioisi tōn stratiōtōn tessarsin ('four squads of four soldiers each'): a quaternio was the standard Roman guard unit of four soldiers, rotating through four six-hour watches. Sixteen soldiers total for one prisoner was exceptional security — reflecting either Herod's determination not to lose another apostle or his knowledge of the earlier miraculous prison release (Acts 5:19-20). The explicit plan — 'public trial after Passover' (meta to pascha) — shows Herod respecting the festival's solemnity while planning a theatrical execution to follow it, using the holy season as stage-setting for political theater.
5: Ektenōs ginomenē hypo tēs ekklēsias ('earnestly/fervently being made by the church'): the adverb ektenōs (stretched out, fervent) appears in Luke 22:44 for Jesus's prayer in Gethsemane ('praying more earnestly'). The verbal echo is available: Peter imprisoned during Passover, the church praying earnestly — as Jesus prayed before his Passover arrest. The prayer is 'to God' (pros ton theon) — specific directed petitionary prayer, not general spiritual posture. The structural contrast Luke creates: Roman military power (four squads, iron gate, Acts 12:4, Acts 12:10) versus the church's prayer (Acts 12:5) — with the outcome demonstrating which is more powerful.
6: Desmios halysesin dysin ('bound with two chains'): Roman custodia militaris — chained at each wrist to a soldier on either side. Peter sleeping (koimōmenos) the night before his trial echoes both Jesus sleeping in the boat during the storm (Mark 4:38 — trust in God amid danger) and the disciples sleeping in Gethsemane (Mark 14:37-41 — the contrasting failure). Peter's sleep signals Lukan theology of apostolic confidence: the man scheduled for execution tomorrow morning sleeps soundly. The imprisonment during Passover, the unjust condemnation, the imminent death — all frame Peter's situation as a Passion pattern which divine rescue will interrupt.
7: Epataxen de tēn pleuran tou Petrou ('struck him on the side'): the angel wakes Peter physically — a prod on the side, not a symbolic gesture. The light (phōs) flooding the cell recalls the divine glory of theophany throughout Acts and the OT (Ezekiel 1:4; Isaiah 60:1). The chains 'fell off' (exepesan) — no struggle, no keys, no human intervention. The passive construction emphasizes divine agency. The same verb is used for Paul's chains in Acts 16:26. Notably, the same verb epataxen (struck) will describe the angel's action against Herod in Acts 12:23 — the word that rescues the apostle kills the persecutor.
8: Zōsai kai hupodēsai ta sandalia ('fasten your belt and put on your sandals'): the practical dress-sequence mirrors a traveler preparing for immediate departure. The specificity — clothes, sandals, cloak — is a realism marker: visions in Acts tend toward symbolic imagery; this scene has the textures of reported experience. The command sequence recalls the Passover preparation instruction in Exodus 12:11 ('eat it with your cloak tucked in, your sandals on your feet and your staff in your hand'). The Exodus echo is available: just as Israel dressed to leave Egypt at God's command, Peter dresses to leave Herod's prison at the angel's command.
9: Edokei de horama blepein ('he thought he was seeing a vision'): the confusion between vision and reality recurs in Acts (Ananias in Acts 9:10; Cornelius in Acts 10:3; Peter in Acts 10:10-16). The disorientation confirms that this experience exceeds ordinary expectation; Peter cannot immediately categorize what is happening. The narrative allows the reader to know what Peter does not: the rescue is real. The retrospective recognition in Acts 12:11 ('now I know without a doubt') parallels the Emmaus road disciples (Luke 24:31-35) — recognition comes after the event, not during it.
10: Automate hē pylinē ēnoigē autois ('the iron gate opened automatically'): automatos ('of its own accord,' without visible human agency) is the term for miraculous openings in Greek literature and in Acts 5:19 (the earlier prison release). The iron gate (pylinē sidēra) was the outer security gate — the final barrier. Its self-opening marks the end of Roman jurisdiction over Peter. 'The length of one street' grounds the story geographically before the angel departs, leaving Peter to navigate by himself. The transition from miraculous assistance to ordinary human navigation is characteristic of Luke's rescue narratives — the divine acts in ways that leave human agency intact.
11: En heautō genomenos ('coming to himself'): the idiom for the return of full self-awareness after disorientation — the same phrase used for the prodigal son's moment of clarity in Luke 15:17. Nun oida alēthōs ('now I know truly'): the transition from confused participant to theological interpreter. Peter's interpretation — 'the Lord has sent his angel and rescued me from Herod's clutches' — connects the rescue to the pattern of angelic rescue throughout Israel's history (Daniel 3:28; Acts 6:22; Psalms 34:7). The phrase 'everything the Jewish people were hoping would happen' shows Peter's awareness of the coordinated opposition between Herod and the Jerusalem leadership.
12: Oikian Marias tēs mētros Iōannou tou epikaloumenou Markou ('the house of Mary the mother of John called Mark'): the house was large enough for a significant gathering and wealthy enough to have a servant named Rhoda — a prosperous Jerusalem household. John Mark (companion of Paul and Barnabas, and per 1 Pet 5:13 Peter's interpreter and associate) is introduced through his mother — grounding the later narrative in a specific household's history. The gathering for prayer (Acts 12:5) was apparently in Mary's house; the community assembled there knew it as a prayer gathering point. The reader now knows where the praying community of Acts 12:5 was located — and that the prayer is answered to the very house where prayer was being made.
13: Paidiskē onomati Rhodē ('a servant girl named Rhoda'): Rhoda (meaning 'rose') is the only named servant in Acts and one of the few named women who is not a major theological figure. The scene she initiates (Acts 12:13-16) is one of Acts' few comic interludes — a recognizable human moment of excitement overriding good sense. Servant girls in ancient Mediterranean households were typically enslaved; Rhoda's central role in the narrative and the detail of her name suggests she was known to the community for whom Luke wrote. Her function in the narrative is to be the first to recognize the answered prayer — before any of the praying congregation does.
14: Apo tēs charas ouk ēnoixen tēn pylōna ('from joy she did not open the gate'): the irony is deliberate — joy at recognizing Peter's voice prevents the obvious response of letting him in. Chara (joy) is Luke's consistent word for the community's response to God's saving acts (Luke 1:14; Acts 2:10; Acts 10:17; Acts 15:7, 10, 32; Acts 8:8; Acts 13:52; Acts 15:3). The structural irony reverses the prison scenario: Peter was inside a locked space and got out; now he is outside a locked space and cannot get in. The gate that should open for the rescued prisoner remains shut while Rhoda runs to announce the news.
15: Ho angelos estin autou ('it must be his angel'): the belief in personal guardian angels — that prominent figures (and perhaps all righteous individuals) had angelic counterparts — was attested in Jewish tradition (Exodus 23:20; Daniel 10:13; Matthew 18:10, 'their angels in heaven always see the face of my Father'). The community's assumption is that Peter has been executed (while they prayed for his release) and his angel-double has come to announce the death. Their 'faith' in the resurrection of the dead (which they affirm theologically) does not extend to believing the servant girl's report that the living Peter is at the gate — a pattern Luke uses throughout: the resurrection and miraculous deliverance always exceed the expectation of those who believe in them abstractly.
16: Epeimenen krouōn ('he kept on knocking'): the imperfect shows persistent action — Peter knocked continuously while the community debated. Their astonishment (exestēsan) when they finally opened the door is the same word used for the crowds at Pentecost (Acts 2:7) and other miraculous events in Acts. Luke's theological point is pointed: the church was praying earnestly for Peter's release, and when the answer to that prayer knocks on their door, they are astonished. Answered prayer, in Luke's narrative, consistently exceeds the faith of those who prayed.
17: Eipate Iakōbō kai tois adelphois ('tell James and the brothers'): Peter's instruction to report to James — not to the Twelve — marks the formal transition of Jerusalem church leadership. James the Lord's brother (Galatians 1:19; Acts 2:9, 12) will be the Jerusalem leader from this point forward (Acts 15:13; Acts 21:18). Peter's departure 'to another place' (eis heteron topon) is deliberately vague — Luke either did not know the destination, declined to name it to protect Peter, or both. Tradition connects this with Rome; whatever the destination, it marks Peter's exit from the Jerusalem narrative and the beginning of James's central role.
18: Tarachos ouk oligos ('no small commotion'): Lukan litotes — 'not small' = very great. Roman law required that guards who permitted a prisoner to escape were liable to the prisoner's sentence — if the prisoner faced execution, so did the guards. This legal consequence explains the commotion: sixteen soldiers face the death penalty for Peter's absence. The commotion is Luke's realistic counterpoint to the miraculous rescue — the world responds to Peter's absence with appropriate institutional panic, even as Peter is safely housed at Mary's home.
19: Anakrinas tous phylakas ekeleusen apachthēnai ('cross-examined the guards and ordered them led away'): the judicial term anakrinō (examine, interrogate) is the same word Paul uses in 1 Cor 4:3 for self-examination before God. The guards' execution is narrated in a subordinate clause without editorial comment — Luke records Roman military justice neutrally, as he records Roman administrative decisions throughout Acts. Herod's move to Caesarea closes the Jerusalem episode; Caesarea was the Roman administrative capital and Herod's preferred residence, offering easier access to Mediterranean communications.
20: Thymomachōn Tyrioisi kai Sidōnioisi ('bitterly angry with the people of Tyre and Sidon'): Tyre and Sidon were commercial rather than agricultural centers — dependent on the grain of the Galilean and Judean interior. 'Food supply from the royal territory' (trephesthai apo tēs basililikēs chōras) reflects the agricultural geography: the Phoenician coast fed on the hinterland. Securing Blastus's support (peisantes Blaston) was the standard patronage mechanism for gaining royal access — the king's personal household staff controlled access to the king's person. The political dispute and its commercial stakes set the stage for the theatrical public address of Acts 12:21.
21: Basilikēn esthēta endysas ('wearing his royal robes'): Josephus (Ant. 19.8.2) describes the same event: at dawn on the second day of games at Caesarea held in honor of the emperor, Herod appeared wearing a robe made entirely of silver, which caught the early morning sun and dazzled the crowd. Luke's 'royal robes' and Josephus's silver robe describe the same theatrical spectacle. The corroboration between Acts and Josephus at this point — independent sources giving the same event with consistent details — is one of the strongest external confirmations of Acts' historical reliability.
22: Theou phōnē kai ouk anthrōpou ('voice of a god, not of a mortal'): the divine acclamation of living rulers was standard in Hellenistic ruler-cult practice. In the Greek East crowds regularly addressed rulers as divine — especially those connected to the imperial household. The Herodians generally avoided accepting divine titles in Jewish contexts while exploiting them in Hellenistic ones. The crowd's shout is comprehensible in its Caesarean Greco-Roman context; the problem is not their pagan enthusiasm but Herod's failure to deflect it to God (Acts 12:23, 'he did not give praise to God'). The same God who heard the church's prayer (Acts 12:5) and sent the rescuing angel now sends the judging one.
23: Exairetōs hypo angelou kyriou ... egeneto skōlēkobrōtos ('struck down by an angel of the Lord ... eaten by worms'): Josephus (Ant. 19.8.2) records Agrippa's sudden illness at the same Caesarean games, describing him looking up and seeing an owl perched on a rope (an omen of death), then suffering violent abdominal pain and dying five days later, attributing it to divine punishment for accepting divine honor. Luke and Josephus agree on: the Caesarean games, the theatrical setting, the sudden illness, and divine causation. The 'eaten by worms' (skōlēkobrōtos) matches the description of Antiochus Epiphanes's death in 2 Macc Acts 9:5-9 — a deliberate literary parallel: both persecutors of God's people die under divine judgment by the same gruesome means.
24: Ho de logos tou theou ēuxanen kai eplēthyneto ('the word of God continued to grow and multiply'): the sixth of Luke's growth summaries in Acts (Acts 2:41, 47; Acts 4:4; Acts 5:14; Acts 6:7; Acts 9:31), each following opposition. The vocabulary — auxanō (grow) and plēthynō (multiply) — echoes Genesis 1:28 (creation mandate: 'be fruitful and multiply') and Exodus 1:7 LXX (Israel's growth in Egypt: 'increased greatly and multiplied'). The irony is pointed: in the same narrative unit where the king who tried to stop the Word dies by divine judgment, the Word does exactly what Pharaoh's Egypt could not stop — it multiplies. The growth summary closes the Agrippa episode and reopens the mission narrative.
25: Iōannēn ton epikaloumenon Markon ('John called Mark'): John Mark's introduction as companion prepares for the first missionary journey (Acts 13:5) and his later departure (Acts 13:13), which will cause conflict between Paul and Barnabas (Acts 15:36-41). Luke introduces Mark neutrally here — as he introduced Saul at Stephen's stoning (Acts 7:58) — as a minor character whose significance will emerge. The parallel is available: Saul watched Stephen's death approvingly; John Mark will depart at a critical moment. Both introductions are understated; both characters will be central to the narrative's next phases. Plērōsantes tēn diakonian: the famine-relief mission (Acts 11:27-30) is complete; the first missionary journey begins.
